methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series G → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.25x) to $252.6B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

data.narrative:
ARC Clean Technology is a Series G nuclear energy company that has raised $50M+ to date. The valuation universe comprises 3 public peers (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private competitors including TerraPower, X-Energy, Kairos Power, and Nano Nuclear Energy.

Given the early-stage nature of the sector and the absence of a sufficient matched peer cohort at the same stage and valuation tier, only one triangulation method yielded reliable results. The Stage-Progression approach applied a 1.25x step-up multiplier typical of Series G advancement to a $252.6B total-raised proxy, producing an implied valuation of $315.8B. Peer-Median methodology was not applicable: while 8 same-sector peers exist, none satisfied the narrow filters (same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar tier), and a broad sector median would introduce excessive noise given the heterogeneity of business models and technology readiness across the nuclear energy landscape.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $268.4B (low) to $363.1B (high), with a central estimate of $315.8B. Key uncertainties include the validity of using total capital raised as a valuation proxy in a sector where cash burn, pathway to commercialization, and regulatory risk vary substantially across competitors. The narrow methodological foundation (one method) reflects genuine data constraints rather than convergent conviction. Peer comparability remains the primary limiting factor for narrowing this range.
